title: Cat Green Mucus From Eye: Causes, Urgency, and Next Steps
meta_desc: Noticed green discharge in your cat's eye? Learn why this indicates infection, the urgency level, and how to triage your pet's eye health.
content_html:
TL;DR: Green eye discharge in cats typically indicates a bacterial infection that requires a veterinary examination within 24 to 48 hours to prevent potential vision loss.

What does it mean if my cat has green or yellow mucus in their eye?

Green or yellow mucus leaking from a cat's eye is typically a sign of a secondary bacterial infection. Unlike clear discharge, which can be caused by allergies or dust, thick and colored mucus indicates that the immune system is actively fighting an underlying issue such as feline conjunctivitis, a corneal ulcer, or a respiratory infection.

Is green discharge from my cat's eye considered a medical emergency?

  • The urgency level for green eye discharge is classified as Medium, requiring a veterinary exam within 24 to 48 hours.
  • It is not usually a life-threatening emergency if your cat is otherwise acting normally, but prompt care is necessary as eye conditions deteriorate quickly.
  • Leaving infections untreated can lead to painful corneal ulcers or permanent vision loss.

How can a photo of my cat's eye help the triage process?

  • Providing a clear, close-up photo of the affected eye helps a veterinary professional assess the severity of the inflammation.
  • A photo allows the triage team to see if the eye is squinting, if the third eyelid is visible, or if there is swelling in the surrounding tissues.
  • This visual information is vital for determining if your cat needs an immediate urgent care appointment or if it can wait until the next available routine slot.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

In cats, green mucus discharge from the eye is often associated with conjunctivitis. Common infectious causes include feline herpesvirus-1, Chlamydia, and Mycoplasma. Chlamydial conjunctivitis should be differentiated from conjunctivitis caused by feline herpesvirus-1 and feline calicivirus. A definitive diagnosis is best confirmed using PCR for chlamydial DNA on conjunctival swabs. Treatment is largely symptomatic and supportive, but broad-spectrum antibiotics are useful against secondary bacterial invaders. Because systemic diseases frequently affect the eye and associated structures, ocular signs can indicate underlying conditions such as feline infectious peritonitis, toxoplasmosis, feline immunodeficiency virus, and feline leukemia virus.

Chapter: Infectious Disease, Ophthalmology

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 1482)

faqs.question: Can I use human eye drops on my cat?
faqs.answer: No. Many human eye drops contain ingredients that are toxic to cats or can cause severe irritation. Only use medications specifically prescribed by your veterinarian.

faqs.question: How can I keep my cat comfortable while waiting for the vet?
faqs.answer: You can gently wipe away the discharge using a soft cloth or cotton ball dampened with lukewarm water. Do not attempt to flush the eye or use over-the-counter ointments.

faqs.question: Could this be an allergy instead of an infection?
faqs.answer: Allergies usually cause clear, watery discharge and itching. Once the discharge becomes thick and green or yellow, it almost certainly indicates a bacterial component that requires medical treatment.
